evals.name: naming

assertions.description: setStatusToClosed renamed to something intent-based like closeAccount
get/set or open/close pairs used consistently
isNotActive replaced with positive boolean like isActive
Single-letter vars (d, f, ms, sz, usr, res, amt, r, a, u) replaced with full words
ms renamed to durationMs or similar with unit suffix
Abbreviations like proc, usr, acct, amt, buf expanded

evals.name: control-flow

assertions.description: Error cases handled with early returns at the top
No more than 3 levels of nesting
Pattern matching or map used where applicable instead of if/else
isUrgent flag replaced with two functions or options object
Mutation of order.status replaced with immutable approach or return value
fetchInventory and fetchPricing run in parallel (Promise.all or similar)

evals.name: functions

assertions.description: No single function exceeds 30 lines
Calculation/formatting logic extracted as pure functions (no I/O)
Fetch, calculate, format, email, cache separated into distinct functions
12 args replaced with options object
Report generation (query) separated from email sending (command)
No class hierarchy, uses function composition
Clear types/interfaces for inputs and outputs
Dependencies (db, cache, logger, mailer) injected, not hard-imported

evals.name: data-types

assertions.description: No mutation of cart or item objects (item.shippingCost, cart.totalShipping)
50, 20, 0.5, 15, 0.3, 8, 5, 1.0, 1.3, 2.5 extracted as named constants
any type on cart replaced with explicit type/interface
Shipping rates/thresholds externalized as config, not inline
Input validation on weight, country, couponCode

evals.name: error-handling

assertions.description: Empty catch blocks and console.log-only catches eliminated
ALL errors (expected and unexpected) returned as Result type, no throw anywhere
Original error preserved as cause when wrapping
Timeouts specified on fetch calls and db queries

evals.name: comments

assertions.description: Exported function has JSDoc with plain block description and @example with return value
Non-obvious decisions (like using sha256+salt vs base64) explained with why
Multi-stage logic has numbered step comments (// 1. ..., // 2. ...)
WARNING or NOTE about salt not being persisted / token not verifiable
Trivial comments removed (// Import dependencies, // Create the hash, etc.)
Section dividers (// --- Constants ---, // --- Types ---) removed
Commented-out old implementation removed
Bare TODO/FIXME without tracking issues removed or flagged

evals.name: file-structure

assertions.description: Recommends putting exports/public API at top, private helpers at bottom
Recommends tests next to source files, not in separate tests/ directory
Recommends users/, orders/, products/ instead of controllers/, models/, services/

evals.name: architecture

assertions.description: Recommends splitting into feature modules (orders/, inventory/)
Recommends injecting db via factory instead of hard-importing
Recommends extracting logging/tracing as wrapping concern
Recommends structured error format (type, code, status, detail) instead of bare message strings
Recommends generating API spec from code or defining schema first

evals.name: project-hygiene
evals.prompt:
I'm starting a new TypeScript backend project. Here's my plan:

1. Write all the features first, add tests later when we have time
2. Use permissive input validation — we'll tighten it if we get abuse
3. We currently use Express but some endpoints are being migrated to Fastify. Both frameworks coexist.
4. No linter configured yet, we'll add one when the codebase is bigger
5. Some devs use `latest` for npm packages to always stay up to date
6. We'll add backward compatibility layers so old clients keep working as we evolve the API

Review this plan and tell me what's wrong.

assertions.description: Flags 'tests later' and 'linter later' — must be from day 1
Flags permissive-first validation — should constrain first, relax later
Flags Express/Fastify coexistence — migrate all-at-once or not at all
Flags missing linter — structural guardrails over human discipline
Flags backward compatibility layers — hard cutover, no compat debt
Flags 'latest' for npm packages — pin all versions

evals.name: comment-quality
evals.expected_output: Comments explain terms in plain language, not just paraphrase function names

assertions.description: Step comments explain what happens in non-expert words, not just paraphrase the function name. '// Read watermark' is a FAIL. '// Read the cursor — where did we stop last time?' is a PASS

ALL domain/technical terms explained on first use: dangling postings, phantom entries, materiality threshold, tolerance bps (basis points), variance. If any term is used in a comment without being defined for a non-expert, it's a FAIL

Comments answer WHY (why this code exists, what breaks without it) not WHAT (what the next line does). '// Apply corrections to the ledger' = FAIL (restates code). '// Apply corrections — without this, the frontend shows stale failure badges' = PASS

When code deliberately does nothing (empty branch, early return for clean state), the comment explains why inaction is correct. 'if corrections empty return clean' without explanation = FAIL. 'Nothing to fix — ledger is already balanced' = PASS
When code mutates shared/persistent state, the comment explains the effect on the next invocation. 'Advance cursor' = FAIL. 'Advance cursor so the next tick only looks at newer deliveries' = PASS

evals.name: comment-next-caller
evals.expected_output: advanceCursor comment explains effect on next tick

assertions.description: The comment on advanceCursor explains what the next call to tick() will see — e.g. 'so the next tick starts from here' or 'next invocation skips these events'. Just 'advance the cursor' = FAIL

evals.name: full-sweep
evals.expected_output: Full refactoring covering all coding-standards rules

assertions.description: Module/function starts with doc comment: what it does + how it works
Comments explain consequences, not mechanics. 'cache so the frontend...' not just 'update cache'
Any domain terms explained on first use
Conversational comment tone, not mechanical
If any no-op/early-return, justified with why
Cache/DB state mutations explain what next caller sees
Return value documented: what the caller should do with it
The 1000 threshold for big orders explained with why
Exported function has JSDoc with block description + @example with return value
Any gotcha or non-obvious behavior flagged with WARNING/NOTE
handleOrderData renamed — no banned words (handle, data, process). Full words (usr→user, itm→item, ct→count, amt→amount, s→stock)
Booleans use is/has/should prefix in positive form
Ambiguous time/size values get unit suffix
Naming pairs consistent (get/set, add/remove)
isUrgent and sendEmail boolean params split into separate functions or options object
Nested if/else flattened to guard clauses, max 3 indent levels
discountCode if/else chain replaced with object map or switch
No mutation of items array or item objects
Independent stock checks run in parallel (Promise.all)
db, cache, mailer injected via factory, not imported as globals
No single function exceeds 30 lines — logic split into helpers
7 positional args replaced with options object
Order creation (command) separated from notification (side-effect)
Magic numbers (1000, 50, 20) extracted to named constants
Explicit types/interfaces for all params and returns, no any
Discount codes/thresholds in config, not inline
Input validation rejects invalid values (negative qty, empty items) — no ?? fallback
Errors returned as Result, no throw
Errors preserve original cause/context
Compound expressions (tax calc, discount) extracted to named variables
Nested ternary for discounts removed, replaced with clear logic
Logical blocks separated with blank lines
Commented-out old approach removed
TODO/FIXME without tracking issue removed
If any conditional branches, narrated with journey (optional — depends on refactoring approach)
